heading: Sharding Collections for Global Writes
body:
For global clusters with [Atlas-Managed Sharding](atlas/tutorial/create-global-cluster)
enabled, unsharded collections must meet the following compatibility
requirements *prior to sharding* to use Global Writes when
sharded:

- Every document in the collection **must** include a `location`
  field.

- The value of the `location` field **must** be either an
  ISO-3166-1 alpha 2 country code (`"US"`, `"DE"`, `"IN"`) *or* a
  supported ISO-3166-2 subdivision code (`"US-DC"`, `"DE-BE"`,
  `"IN-DL"`). Documents that don't match this criteria can't be routed
  to any shard in the cluster. To view the complete list of
  currently supported country or subdivision codes, see
  [https://cloud.mongodb.com/static/atlas/country\_iso\_codes.txt](https://cloud.mongodb.com/static/atlas/country_iso_codes.txt).

A shard key on the `location` field alone might result in bottlenecks,
especially for workloads where a subset of countries or subdivisions
receive the majority of write operations. Atlas Global Writes
requires a [compound shard key](manual/core/index-compound) to
facilitate the efficient distribution of sharded data across the
cluster. Atlas Global Cluster shard keys share the same
restrictions as MongoDB shard keys. The following Atlas
Global Cluster limitations apply:

- The first field of the compound shard key must be `location` and
  *can't* be hashed.

- There can only be one secondary shard key field in a compound shard
  key.

- The secondary shard key field of a compound shard key *can* be hashed.

- The secondary shard key field *can't* be an array.

To learn more about:

- Choosing a secondary shard key field and the effect of shard key choices
  on data distribution, see [Choosing a Shard Key](manual/core/sharding-choose-a-shard-key).

- Shard key limitations, see [Shard Key Limitations](manual/reference/limits).

- Available hashed sharding options, see [Hashed Sharding Options](global-writes-initial-chunks).

> **Important:** 
>
> Changing the Shard Key
>
> After sharding, what you can modify depends on the version of
> MongoDB that you run:
>
> | MongoDB Version | Modify Shard Key Keys                             | Modify Shard Key Values |
> | --------------- | ------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------- |
> | MongoDB 7.0     | Yes, add fields to a key using the Atlas UI only. | Yes                     |
> | MongoDB 8.0     | Yes, add fields to a key using the Atlas UI only. | Yes                     |
>
> In all MongoDB versions, you can't [reshard a collection](manual/core/sharding-reshard-a-collection) on a Global Cluster.

The [Atlas UI](atlas-ui) supports creating sharded
collections with specific validations for Global Writes.
To learn more, see [Atlas UI](de-shard-collection-for-global-writes).

You can also use [mongosh](mongodb-shell) to execute the
[sh.shardCollection()](manual/reference/method/sh.shardCollection). After sharding
the collection, you must use the [Atlas UI](atlas-ui)
to enable Global Writes for that collection.

WARNING: If you shard a collection that already has a MongoDB Search
index, you might experience a brief period of incomplete search results
while the index is building on a shard. Also, if you add a shard for
an already sharded collection that contains a MongoDB Search index, your search
queries against that collection might return incomplete results until
the initial sync process completes on the added shards. To learn more,
see [initial sync process](troubleshoot-initial-sync).

To learn more about sharding collections via the Atlas UI, see
[Atlas UI](de-shard-collection-for-global-writes).
